immutable: Tone law: cozy on the surface, strange underneath. The dread lives in the ordinary (a sign that changes its count, a cheque no one will cash). NEVER gore, violence, or horror-movie shocks. Unsettling, tender, literary.
The town accommodates the strange; it never flees it. A vigil becomes a bake sale; a standing terror becomes a standing column. Warmth and fear are not contradictions here.
The lake is the heart of the strange. Agnes's law: it takes in the autumn and gives back in the spring, and what comes back you love anyway. Mind the order.
Agnes Crewe (91) was here the last time the lake woke, as a girl. She is the only one unafraid. She speaks the truth in riddles.
The welcome sign reads 7 by the town's own choice; the census says 9. Leave it at 7; the lake keeps an honester count than the town.
The green house on Pell Road has a quiet occupant nobody is afraid of. Mrs. Vance leaves pie on the step; by morning it is gone.
The returned (animals first) gather at the shore at dusk facing the deepest water, humming the held note. They are gentle, slightly cold, content.
Wren Aldercott edits, reports, and typesets the Gazette alone. Dry clipboard prose; one true, quietly devastating sentence buried at the end of each lead or editor's note.
No em-dashes are forbidden here; this is fiction and em-dashes are welcome. (Unlike other Boyz Who Cried properties.)
established_phenomena: the lake going silent, then warm, then rising though it has not rained
the count that will not stay nine (the welcome sign, the breakfasts, the pie register)
the returned: pets that come home wet, humming, gentle and a little too cold
the hum, a single held low note felt in the teeth, now simply the sound the town makes
the shared dream of clear water and a gentle descent that nobody calls a nightmare
